title: Foot care and blister prevention
summary: Protect feet during long walking, cleanup, shelter work, and wet conditions.
content: Foot problems can stop movement, cleanup, and caregiving. Keep feet dry, rotate socks, inspect pressure points, and deal with rubbing early. Do not cut blisters casually or walk on infected-looking wounds. People with diabetes, poor circulation, numbness, or immune problems need extra caution and qualified care when possible. For Foot care and blister prevention, the useful record should include the date, the person responsible, what was checked, what stayed uncertain, and the next safe action. Review foot care and blister prevention after weather changes, illness changes, new damage, new supplies, or movement to another place, because small changes can make an old decision unsafe.
materials: Notebook. Context: “Foot care and blister prevention”.
Pencil. Context: “Foot care and blister prevention”.
Labels. Context: “Foot care and blister prevention”.
Clean storage space. Context: “Foot care and blister prevention”.
steps: Change wet socks as soon as possible.
Reduce rubbing with better lacing, padding, or rest.
Keep small wounds clean and covered.
Record worsening pain, redness, swelling, heat, or drainage.
warnings: Do not ignore foot wounds in people with diabetes or numbness.
Do not share dirty socks or footwear.
common_mistakes: Treating Foot care and blister prevention as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Foot care and blister prevention without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Foot care and blister prevention because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Foot care and blister prevention with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Foot care and blister prevention if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Foot care and blister prevention and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Foot care and blister prevention or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Foot care and blister prevention are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.

The routine ignores non-diagnostic care, warning signs, records, and referral to qualified help and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Foot care and blister prevention”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Foot care and blister prevention as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Foot care and blister prevention when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Foot care and blister prevention.
short_term: For the first hours of Foot care and blister prevention, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Foot care and blister prevention remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Foot care and blister prevention fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Foot care and blister prevention to the real environment: check non-diagnostic care, warning signs, records, and referral to qualified help, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
